expanded_description: Rhombuses and rectangles are special types of parallelograms, each with additional properties beyond those of a general parallelogram. A rhombus is a parallelogram with four congruent sides; its diagonals are perpendicular and bisect the angles. A rectangle is a parallelogram with four right angles; its diagonals are congruent. Both inherit all parallelogram properties. The square, possessing all properties of both, is the intersection of these two special types.

core_concept.principle: A rhombus is a parallelogram with all four sides congruent. Additional properties: diagonals are perpendicular, and diagonals bisect the interior angles. A rectangle is a parallelogram with all four angles right. Additional property: diagonals are congruent. A square is both a rhombus and a rectangle, having all properties of both.
core_concept.explanation: Start with a generic parallelogram. Force all four sides equal—you get a rhombus (diamond). Its diagonals meet at right angles and cut the corner angles in half. Force all four angles to 90°—you get a rectangle. Its diagonals are equal in length. Demand both—all sides equal AND all angles 90°—and you get a square, the most symmetric quadrilateral. Each shape inherits everything from its parallelogram parent and adds its own distinctive diagonal behavior.

image.alt: Venn diagram: Parallelogram containing Rhombus and Rectangle as overlapping subsets, with Square in the intersection. A rhombus with perpendicular diagonals, a rectangle with congruent diagonals, a square with both.
image.caption: Rhombus: perpendicular diagonals. Rectangle: congruent diagonals. Square: both.

sections.introduction: A rhombus is a parallelogram with four congruent sides. This single additional condition produces two powerful diagonal properties not found in general parallelograms. All parallelogram properties apply.

introduction_image.alt: A rhombus with all four sides marked congruent. Parallelogram properties labeled inherited. Perpendicular diagonals and angle bisector properties highlighted.
introduction_image.caption: Rhombus: a parallelogram with four equal sides and special diagonals.

key_concept.definition: A rhombus is a parallelogram with four congruent sides. Two rhombus-specific theorems: (1) The diagonals of a rhombus are perpendicular. (2) Each diagonal bisects a pair of opposite angles. All parallelogram properties hold: opposite sides parallel, opposite angles congruent, consecutive angles supplementary, diagonals bisect each other.
key_concept.context: The perpendicular diagonals are proved using SSS on the four triangles formed by the diagonals. The angle bisector property follows from the same congruent triangles. Area of a rhombus = (1/2)d₁d₂.

image.alt: Rhombus with diagonals. Right angles at intersection marked. Angle bisector arcs at all four vertices.
image.caption: Rhombus: diagonals are perpendicular and bisect the angles.

process_flow.description: Verify all four sides are congruent, or it is a parallelogram with consecutive congruent sides.
process_flow.mechanism: All four sides marked equal, the word 'rhombus' given, or parallelogram with AB ≅ BC.
applications: 'ABCD is a rhombus.' 'AB ≅ BC ≅ CD ≅ DA.'

image.alt: Three ways to identify a rhombus.
image.caption: Step 1: Confirm the quadrilateral is a rhombus.

process_flow.description: Diagonals are perpendicular and bisect the interior angles.
process_flow.mechanism: AC ⟂ BD. Each diagonal splits the angles at its endpoints into two equal parts. Use these to find angle measures, side lengths, or area.
process_flow.result: Angle measures, side lengths, or area found.

image.alt: Rhombus with diagonals. Right triangles formed by half-diagonals. Pythagorean calculation shown.
image.caption: Step 2: Use perpendicular diagonals and angle bisectors.

image.alt: Complete rhombus section with properties and examples.
image.caption: Properties of Rhombuses overview.

sections.introduction: A rectangle is a parallelogram with four right angles. This single condition produces the key property that diagonals are congruent. All parallelogram properties apply.

introduction_image.alt: A rectangle with all four right angles marked. Parallelogram properties inherited. Congruent diagonals highlighted.
introduction_image.caption: Rectangle: a parallelogram with four right angles and congruent diagonals.

key_concept.definition: A rectangle is a parallelogram with four right angles. The Rectangle Diagonal Theorem: The diagonals of a rectangle are congruent. All parallelogram properties hold: opposite sides parallel and congruent, opposite angles congruent (all 90°), consecutive angles supplementary, diagonals bisect each other.
key_concept.context: Congruent diagonals are proved using SAS: the two triangles formed by a diagonal share it, have congruent opposite sides, and have right angles. A parallelogram with one right angle is a rectangle.

image.alt: Rectangle with diagonals drawn and marked congruent. Right angles at all vertices.
image.caption: Rectangle: diagonals are congruent.

process_flow.description: Verify all four angles are right, or it is a parallelogram with one right angle.
process_flow.mechanism: Four right angles marked, 'rectangle' given, or parallelogram with m∠A = 90°.
applications: 'ABCD is a rectangle.' 'ABCD is a parallelogram and m∠A = 90°.'

image.alt: Three ways to identify a rectangle.
image.caption: Step 1: Confirm the quadrilateral is a rectangle.

process_flow.mechanism: AC ≅ BD. Use to set up equations or find lengths. In coordinates, a parallelogram with equal diagonals is a rectangle.

image.alt: Rectangle with congruent diagonals and equation setup.
image.caption: Step 2: Use congruent diagonals.

image.alt: Complete rectangle section with properties and examples.
image.caption: Properties of Rectangles overview.

sections.introduction: A square is both a rhombus and a rectangle. It inherits all properties of both. Practice applying rhombus, rectangle, and square properties.

introduction_image.alt: A square with all properties labeled: congruent sides, right angles, perpendicular diagonals, congruent diagonals, angle bisectors.
introduction_image.caption: Square: all rhombus and rectangle properties combined.

key_concept.definition: A square is a parallelogram with four congruent sides and four right angles. It has all parallelogram, rhombus, and rectangle properties. Diagonals are congruent, perpendicular, and bisect the angles.
key_concept.context: The square is the intersection of rhombus and rectangle sets. It is the most symmetric quadrilateral. If a quadrilateral is both a rhombus and a rectangle, it is a square.

image.alt: Venn diagram showing Square at intersection of Rhombus and Rectangle.
image.caption: Square = Rhombus + Rectangle.

worked_examples.title: Rhombus, Rectangle, and Square Problems
worked_examples.problem:
1) Rhombus: m∠A = 70°. Find m∠DAC.
2) Rectangle: PT = 4x−3, TQ = 2x+7. Find diagonal PR.
3) Rhombus: diagonals 10 and 24. Find side and area.
4) Prove rectangle diagonals are congruent.

step_by_step.explanation: Diagonal bisects ∠A: m∠DAC = 70°/2 = 35°. Diagonals are perpendicular: angle between them is 90°.
PT = TQ (half-diagonals of rectangle are equal). 4x−3 = 2x+7 → x=5. PT = 17. PR = 34.
AD ≅ BC, DC ≅ DC, ∠ADC ≅ ∠BCD (90°). SAS → △ADC ≅ △BCD. AC ≅ BD by CPCTC.

summary: Rhombuses and rectangles are special parallelograms. Rhombus: four congruent sides; diagonals perpendicular and bisect angles. Rectangle: four right angles; diagonals congruent. Both inherit all parallelogram properties. A square is both, possessing all properties. Area of rhombus = (1/2)d₁d₂.

review_questions_image.alt: Venn diagram concept map with Parallelogram, Rhombus, Rectangle, Square, and all properties.
review_questions_image.caption: Properties of Rhombuses and Rectangles Review.
